weather: Edmonds WA, April 23 2026: Partly cloudy, high 55F / low 44F. Showers and slight chance of thunderstorms in the evening, then chance of rain late evening and early morning. Rainfall amounts 0.1–0.25 inches possible, higher amounts possible in thunderstorms.

local_news.headline: Edmonds City Council April 7 meeting recap
local_news.findings: Council approved personnel changes, contract approvals and budget amendments unanimously. Key actions: voluntarily dismissed counterpetition in Redelfs landmark tree case (33-inch tree removed without permit; $102,245 fine levied on Richard Henry Redelfs, tree replanting ordered) — passed 5-1, Councilmember Susan Paine voting no. Budget amendments include advancing money for Edmonds Marsh (awaiting grant for 30% design), park impact fee study, IT support. Mayor Mike Rosen proclaimed April 9 as Local News Day. Council meetings broadcast Tuesdays 7 PM.

Movies in the Park series returning this summer — businesses and community partners invited. Edmonds Waterfront Center hosted multi-generational community events. Edmonds School District Board passed policy changes at April 14 regular meeting.

local_news.headline: Edmonds Police Blotter March 30 – April 5, 2026
local_news.findings: Incidents include: suspicious circumstances at closed business, identity theft, theft at local businesses, disorderly conduct with resisting arrest and assault, DUI arrest. General stats: Edmonds overall crime rate (30.46) below national average (33.37). Violent crime significantly lower (6.06 vs. 13.84 national). Property crime slightly higher (24.4 vs. 19.53 national). 1-in-385 chance of violent crime victimization; 1-in-34 for property crime.

global_news.headline: Trump extends Iran ceasefire; blockade tensions persist
global_news.findings: Trump extended ceasefire with Iran via social media post, pending permanent proposal. US maintaining blockade of Iranian ports/Strait of Hormuz. Iran says ceasefire is meaningless unless blockade lifted — calling it an act of war. Iran collecting some oil tolls in yuan (petrodollar stress). IAEA chief: 'peace without nuclear oversight is an illusion.' JD Vance remained in Washington for 'additional policy meetings' instead of leading Iran talks.

global_news.headline: FBI investigating deaths and disappearances of 10+ scientists linked to government projects
global_news.findings: FBI coordinating with DOE, Pentagon, and local law enforcement investigating potential connections between deaths/disappearances of 10+ scientists. Includes retired Air Force Maj. Gen. William Neil McCasland (disappeared from Albuquerque Feb 2026 with gun and wallet). MIT professor Nuno F.G. Loureiro fatally shot near Boston Dec 2025. House Oversight Committee conducting separate inquiry. No concrete connections established beyond government ties and incidents post-2022.

Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer departing (third cabinet departure, was under internal investigation). Navy Secretary John Phelan departed after 13 months amid Pentagon tensions; Undersecretary Hung Cao acting. Fed pick Kevin Warsh before Senate committee.

triadic_ontology.findings: Active scholarly series: 'Studies on Triadic Ontology and Trinitarian Philosophy' (Verlag Karl Alber), edited by Markus Enders, Emmanuel Falque, John Milbank, Piero Coda, and others. Active conference group: TRIERTIUM (2025-2026). Matteo Raffaelli published in Modern Theology (Feb 2026) on Trinitarian ontology and relational metaphysics. Core thesis: the Triune God as ontological ground of relationality — being is inherently relational because God is relation. Perichoresis as structural model for relational and infinitely divisible metaphysical structures. PhilArchive papers: 'Trilism: A Triadic Systems Theory for Resolving the Hard Problem' (connecting triadic structures to philosophy of mind); 'Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics.' Triadic Metaphysics: Spinoza's Expression as Structural Ontology (ResearchGate).

ai_systems.findings: Dominant 2026 trend: enterprises moving from chatbots to multi-agent orchestration — specialized agents collaborating as 'digital teams.' Multi-model routing now standard: frontier models for reasoning-heavy tasks, lighter models for high-volume subtasks, reducing costs 40-60%. Architecture emphasis: separate reactive from deliberative systems, structured JSON-schema communication between agents (replacing free-text), observability, principled conflict resolution. Leading frameworks: LangGraph, CrewAI. arXiv cs.MA active with new papers April 2026. Core maxim: 'Agentic AI success is about smarter architecture, not smarter models.' OpenAI regrouping internal teams for voice-first hardware.

uap.findings: 119th Congress H.R.1187 UAP Transparency Act: requires President to direct all federal agencies to declassify UAP records and publish on agency websites. Joint House Oversight hearing 'Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena: Exposing the Truth' (Subcommittees on Cybersecurity/IT/Government Innovation and National Security/Border/Foreign Affairs) — focused on DoD/IC lack of transparency. Separate hearing: 'Restoring Public Trust Through UAP Transparency and Whistleblower Protection' — examined AARO transparency failures and whistleblower protection mechanisms. FY2026 NDAA requires Pentagon to brief Congress on UAP intercept operations since 2004 involving integrated military commands (North American defense focus). Connecticut state HB-05422 introduced Feb 2026. Maj. Gen. McCasland (ret.) — linked to To the Stars organization — disappeared Feb 2026; included in FBI scientist investigation.

newyorker.title: When Soul Food Met Daniel Boulud
newyorker.dek: The Harlem franchise Charles Pan-Fried Chicken invited a bunch of chefs to take over for the weekend. Up next: oxtails from Lana Lagomarsini. Zach Helfand writes.
newyorker.author: Zach Helfand
newyorker.text:
Last week, Quié, who has the build of a refrigerator and the energy of a hummingbird, was at the Charles Pan-Fried Chicken on 145th Street, preparing for another collaboration. He was inviting celebrated chefs to take over for one weekend each month. The meals are open to anyone, to-go, and cost twenty-five dollars. "It's not a money-maker. At all," Quié said. "But it's not about that. It's like when Jay-Z brought Oprah to Brooklyn."
This weekend's chef, Lana Lagomarsini, met Quié at a picnic table outside. She was there to test her dish: tamarind barbecue-glazed oxtails with chowchow, which would pair with Quié's Rasta pasta. Lagomarsini, a semifinalist on "Top Chef," has worked at Gramercy Tavern, Momofuku Ko, and Francis Mallmann's Garzón, in Uruguay. She joined an ongoing lineup—ten chefs so far—that has included Ayo Balogun, of the Dept of Culture, in Bed-Stuy, and Serigne Mbaye, whose New Orleans restaurant, Dakar NOLA, is considered among the best in the country. "This is like the Justice League," Quié said.
Lagomarsini laughed. "I'm just regular-degular."
"No, you are in the catalogue of dopeness," Quié said.
Lagomarsini, who wore gold earrings and has arm tattoos, got organized. Quié kept getting up, sitting down, calling out to people in the street, checking on the kitchen, and whipping out his phone to film promotional videos. Lagomarsini seemed used to it: chefs.
She lives nearby. "I come here myself once a week," she said. "There's a three-piece, you get two sides, and it's eighteen bucks. It's very shareable, but also not shareable, you know?" Lagomarsini's cooking celebrates the Black diaspora. "I get a kick out of tracking down recipes of old Black chefs," she said. She has unearthed dishes by Thomas Downing, known in the nineteenth century as the Oyster King of New York, and by James Hemings, born into slavery, who was Thomas Jefferson's chef in France and helped introduce America to macaroni and cheese. Lagomarsini also discovered material about her ancestors. "I found out for sure my family was enslaved," she said. "I found the name of our owners, and where the family is. They're still alive." This summer, she's opening her first restaurant, a fourteen-seat supper club on the Upper East Side. She named it Creola, after her grandmother.
Quié popped out of the kitchen and said, "Showtime!" There were four burners going. Quié had prepped Lagomarsini's dishes and was working on an Alfredo sauce. "I based it off Olive Garden's," he said. They assessed the oxtails: too small. "I want huge oxtails for the weekend," Quié said.
The final side dish was Gabriel's cornbread, made with plantains and drizzled with cane-sugar-infused honey. Growing up in Harlem, Quié idolized Gabriel, the son of sharecroppers and one of twenty kids. "Same parents, by the way," Quié said. "Everyone had chores. His job was to catch the chicken, kill the chicken, pluck the chicken." During the pandemic, Quié was furloughed from a Dave & Buster's. He approached Gabriel, who is seventy-eight, about cooking for him. Gabriel told him he'd had to close the restaurant. "I was, like, 'Nah,' " Quié said. They partnered to bring it back.
Lagomarsini slathered the oxtails with the glaze, which was dangerously sticky. "It's a blessing and a curse," she said. "One time it spilled all over the trunk of my car." Quié attended to two burners while running around recording Lagomarsini on his phone. Lagomarsini dolloped more glaze and said, "Want me to plate up, chef?"
She displayed the dish as Quié directed her through a promo. "How do I say this properly—you're gonna describe it sexy-like," he said. They returned to the picnic table to taste. Lagomarsini offered a bite to Quié's nephew Poppa, who works at the restaurant. He shyly declined.
"Boss, please!" Quié said. "Grab a fork." Poppa sat down. "All right, try the oxtail first," Quié said. "Sauce. Now eat the chowchow. Now take another oxtail. Pasta now. O.K., now you can have the cornbread."
Quié was del [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: These Middle Eastern News Sites Are U.S. Government Propaganda Operations
enriched_articles.author: Sam Biddle
enriched_articles.text: The sites — Al-Fassel and Pishtaz News — disclose U.S. funding only in hard-to-find 'About' pages, not on social media platforms, violating platform policies requiring state-backed media labels. Both outlets appear connected to the Trans-Regional Web Initiative (TRWI), a Pentagon psychological operations program started in 2008 by Special Operations Command. After being defunded by Congress in 2014, many TRWI sites rebranded with vague funding disclosures. Content patterns include heavy reliance on CENTCOM press releases, AI-generated news anchors, heavily biased coverage favoring U.S./Israeli positions on Iran and Gaza, and limited substantive reach (Al-Fassel: ~2,400 X followers; Pishtaz: 132 X followers). While technically 'overt' propaganda due to buried disclosures, these sites function deceptively by using journalism as camouflage. Verbatim disclosure language: 'a product of an international media organization publicly funded from the budget of the United States Government.'

enriched_articles.title: FBI Investigating Possible Links Between Deaths and Disappearances of at Least 10 Scientists
enriched_articles.text: The FBI has launched a coordinated investigation into potential connections among at least 10 deaths and disappearances of scientists linked to government projects, following public attention from President Trump and Republican lawmakers. The investigation involves multiple agencies including the Department of Energy, Pentagon, and state/local law enforcement. One prominent case concerns retired Air Force Maj. Gen. William Neil McCasland, who disappeared from his Albuquerque home in February 2026 with his gun and wallet. McCasland's wife countered online speculation, stating it was 'quite unlikely that he was taken to extract very dated secrets from him,' noting her husband had retired 13 years prior. She also dismissed connections to his involvement with To the Stars, an organization promoting theories about aliens and UFOs. Officials emphasize there is currently no concrete evidence connecting the incidents beyond the researchers' government ties and the fact that none occurred before 2022. NASA stated that 'nothing related to NASA indicates a national security threat' and pledged cooperation with investigators. House Oversight Committee also conducting separate inquiry.

enriched_articles.title: Lobbying Firms Took In a Record $5 Billion in 2025
enriched_articles.text: Lobbying spending reached an unprecedented $5.08 billion in 2025, representing a 14 percent increase from the previous year — the largest jump since quarterly disclosures began in 2008. 15,768 organizations lobbied, up nearly 12 percent from 2024. Ballard Partners led all firms with a record $88.1 million in revenue, tripling its 2024 earnings. The firm benefits from leadership ties to the Trump administration, including White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles and Attorney General Pam Bondi as former employees. Brownstein Hyatt and BGR Group followed at approximately $71 million each. Checkmate Government Relations — launched with $70,000 in late 2024 — earned $22.2 million in its first full year, leveraging connections to Trump Jr. and administration insiders. Healthcare led lobbying investment at $868 million, with pharmaceutical and hospital organizations navigating Medicaid cuts. Trade-related lobbying surged 432% as tariff concerns mounted.

enriched_articles.title: Memeorandum top stories — April 23, 2026
enriched_articles.text: Virginia redistricting referendum blocked by court — judge halted certification of voter-approved congressional map benefiting Democrats, ruled referendum and enabling legislation unconstitutional; AG pledged appeal. Navy Secretary John Phelan departed Trump administration after 13 months amid tensions with Pete Hegseth; Undersecretary Hung Cao acting. FBI examined NYT reporter following coverage of Kash Patel's girlfriend; FBI not pursuing charges, raises press freedom concerns. DHS counterterrorism official placed on administrative leave over 'sugar daddy' allegations. Kalshi suspended and fined three congressional candidates for 'political insider trading' on their own campaigns — platform's first enforcement actions.

enriched_articles.title: Texas Banned Abortion. Then Sepsis Rates Soared.
enriched_articles.text: Pregnancy became far more dangerous in Texas after the state banned abortion in 2021. The rate of sepsis shot up more than 50% for women hospitalized when they lost their pregnancies in the second trimester. The surge was most pronounced for patients whose fetus may still have had a heartbeat when they arrived at the hospital. ProPublica previously reported on two such cases where miscarrying women died of sepsis after doctors delayed evacuating their uteruses — doing so would have been considered an abortion. Dozens more pregnant and postpartum women died in Texas hospitals than in pre-pandemic baseline years, as the national maternal mortality rate dropped. ProPublica analyzed seven years of Texas hospital discharge data. 'This is exactly what we predicted would happen and exactly what we were afraid would happen,' said Dr. Lorie Harper, maternal-fetal medicine specialist in Austin. Texas law threatens up to 99 years in prison for providing an abortion. The ban includes a 'medical emergency' exception but its definition has been subject to confusion and debate. The analysis comes as Texas legislators consider amending the abortion ban.
